input_row.title: Ensure that usage is restricted and expiry is enforced for Databricks personal access tokens
input_row.rationale: Databricks personal access tokens (PATs) provide API-based authentication for users and applications. By default, users can generate API tokens without expiration, leading to potential security risks if tokens are leaked, improperly stored, or not rotated regularly. To mitigate these risks, administrators should: • Restrict token creation to approved users and service principals. • Enforce expiration policies to prevent long-lived tokens. • Monitor token usage and revoke unused or compromised tokens.
input_row.audit: Restricting usage and enforcing expiry for personal access tokens reduces exposure to long-lived tokens, minimizes the risk of API abuse if compromised, and aligns with security best practices through controlled issuance and enforced expiry. Impact: If revoked improperly, applications relying on these tokens may fail, requiring a remediation plan for token rotation. Increased administrative effort is required to track and manage API tokens effectively.
input_row.remediation: Azure Databricks administrators can monitor and revoke personal access tokens within their workspace. Detailed instructions are available in the "Monitor and Revoke Personal Access Tokens" section of the Microsoft documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/admin/access-control/tokens. To evaluate the usage of personal access tokens in your Azure Databricks account, you can utilize the provided notebook that lists all PATs not rotated or updated in the last 90 days, allowing you to identify tokens that may require revocation. This process is detailed here: https://docs.azure.cn/en-us/databricks/security/auth/oauth-pat-usage. Implementing diagnostic logging provides a comprehensive reference of audit log services and events, enabling you to track activities related to personal access tokens. More information can be found in the diagnostic log reference section: https://docs.azure.cn/en-us/databricks/security/auth/oauth-pat-usage.
input_row.impact: Remediate from Azure Portal Disable personal access tokens: If your workspace does not require PATs, you can disable them entirely to prevent their use. 1. Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace. 2. Click the Settings icon and select Admin Console. 3. Go to the Advanced tab. 4. Under Personal Access Tokens, toggle the setting to Disabled. Databricks CLI: databricks workspace-conf set-status --json '{"enableTokens": "false"}' Control who can create and use personal access tokens: Define which users or groups are authorized to create and utilize PATs. 1. Navigate to your Azure Databricks workspace. 2. Click the Settings icon and select Admin Console. 3. Go to the Advanced tab. 4. Click on Personal Access Tokens and then Permissions. 5. Assign the appropriate permissions (e.g. No Permissions, Can Use, Can Manage) to users or groups. Set maximum lifetime for new personal access tokens: Limit the validity period of new tokens to reduce potential misuse. Databricks CLI: databricks workspace-conf set-status --json '{"maxTokenLifetimeDays": "90"}' Monitor and revoke personal access tokens: Periodically review active tokens and revoke any that are unnecessary or potentially compromised. Databricks CLI: databricks token list databricks token delete --token-id <token-id> Transition to OAuth for enhanced security: Utilize OAuth tokens for authentication, offering improved security features over PATs. Default Value: By default, personal access tokens are enabled and users can create the Personal access token and their expiry time. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/administration-guide/access- control/tokens 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/dev-tools/auth/
input_row.reference: If revoked improperly, applications relying on these tokens may fail, requiring a remediation plan for token rotation. Increased administrative effort is required to track and manage API tokens effectively.

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit clearly mentions API/CLI commands for monitoring and managing personal access tokens.
- Azure Databricks provides APIs and CLI commands that can be integrated to enforce the policies surrounding personal access tokens.
- The vague areas of the control are addressed in the remediation and references which detail actions that can be taken through the Azure environment.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_databricks_personal_access_tokens_expiry_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- The automated program would check:
  - Utilize the Azure Databricks CLI command `databricks token list` to enumerate personal access tokens in the workspace.
  - Validate the presence and proper lifecycle of each token, specifically checking:
    - Expiry settings for tokens (whether any tokens are set to never expire).
    - Max lifetime configuration for new personal access tokens using `databricks workspace-conf get | jq '.maxTokenLifetimeDays'` and ensure it aligns with an organization-defined value (e.g., 90 days).
  - Check the permissions assigned to users/groups for token creation using `databricks workspace-conf get` to evaluate who has the ability to create tokens.
  
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - Pass: All tokens have an expiration value set, and no tokens have unlimited expiration.
  - Pass: Max token lifetime setting is configured (for example, to 90 days).
  - Pass: Appropriate permissions for token creation are restricted to designated users/groups.
  
- **Example API Endpoints/CLI Commands:**
  - **Enumerate Tokens:** `databricks token list`
  - **Check Token Configuration:** `databricks workspace-conf get` (with appropriate filtering commands).
